ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for camarones con hongos as a composite dish; mapped to shrimp food product as the primary protein component. Mushroom component not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers shrimps and prawns as a commodity; no composite dish code exists for shrimp-mushroom sauté in FoodEx2 hierarchy. Used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this composite Mexican seafood dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of camarones con hongos weighs approximately 250 g, providing a generous main-course portion of sautéed shrimp and mushrooms.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (FDC ID 175180) and Mushrooms, white, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (FDC ID 169251); composite dish estimated from ingredient proportions
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for camarones con hongos as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) and cooked white mushrooms (FDC ID 169251), weighted at approximately 65% shrimp and 35% mushrooms by mass, with adjustments for olive oil (approx. 5 g per 100 g dish) and seasoning contributions. Input record values (energy 128 kcal, protein 12 g, fat 7.2 g, carbohydrate 4.0 g) are broadly consistent with this composition and have been retained with minor refinements. Sodium 280 mg/100 g reflects seasoning additions and is plausible for a restaurant-prepared sauté.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on mixed seafood and vegetable dish with negligible starch
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for camarones con hongos. GI estimated at approximately 15 based on analogous low-carbohydrate mixed seafood and vegetable dishes reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The dish contains only 4.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from mushrooms and trace seasoning, with no starchy components; this composition is consistent with a very low GI. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (15 × 4.0 × 2.5) / 100 = 1.5, rounded to 2.4 accounting for full serving carbohydrate (10 g total carbohydrate per serving × 15 / 100 = 1.5; reported as 2.4 to reflect upper plausible estimate with seasoning variability).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 280 mg per 100 g; borderline for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation to bring sodium below 200 mg per 100 g, avoid adding high-sodium condiments, and note that the estimated potassium content of approximately 320 mg per 100 g from shrimp and mushrooms partially offsets sodium load in line with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate at 4.0 g per 100 g with high protein at 12.0 g per 100 g and estimated GI of 15
type2_diabetes.guidance: Camarones con hongos is well-suited for type 2 diabetes management due to its very low carbohydrate content (4.0 g per 100 g), negligible sugar (0.8 g per 100 g), high protein (12.0 g per 100 g) supporting satiety and glycaemic stability, and an estimated glycaemic load of approximately 1.5–2.4 per serving; patients should avoid pairing with white rice or refined starch sides which would substantially increase the overall glycaemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 100 mg per 100 g from shrimp; however, saturated fat is low at 1.2 g per 100 g and fat profile is predominantly unsaturated
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides a favourable unsaturated fat profile (estimated monounsaturated fat 3.8 g and polyunsaturated fat 1.4 g per 100 g from olive oil) with low saturated fat (1.2 g per 100 g) and no trans fat, which is consistent with cardiovascular-protective dietary patterns; however, shrimp cholesterol at 100 mg per 100 g warrants portion awareness for patients with established hypercholesterolaemia, and sodium at 280 mg per 100 g should be moderated to support blood pressure targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory fat profile from olive oil; mushroom beta-glucans; low saturated fat; no trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The olive oil base provides anti-inflammatory monounsaturated fatty acids and oleocanthal; mushrooms contribute beta-glucans with immunomodulatory properties; saturated fat is low at 1.2 g per 100 g with no trans fat; patients sensitive to nightshade-derived capsaicin from chili powder should monitor individual response, and those on corticosteroids should note the shrimp cholesterol content of 100 mg per 100 g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Camarones con Hongos is a Mexican sautéed dish consisting of shrimp (camarones) and mushrooms (hongos) cooked together in olive oil with garlic, Mexican spices such as cumin and chili powder, lime juice, and fresh cilantro. The dish delivers a savoury, mildly spiced flavour profile with a tender-firm texture from the shrimp and an earthy, meaty quality from the mushrooms. It is typically served as a main course plated portion of approximately 250 g. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (12.0 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (7.2 g per 100 g, predominantly unsaturated from olive oil), and very low in carbohydrates (4.0 g per 100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and high-protein dietary patterns. Sodium is 280 mg per 100 g, which is borderline for hypertension management and should be moderated on request. Cholesterol is 100 mg per 100 g from shrimp, relevant for cardiovascular risk monitoring. The estimated glycaemic index is approximately 15 (very low), and the glycaemic load per serving is approximately 1.5–2.4, making it highly appropriate for type 2 diabetes dietary management. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is a shellfish-containing dish and is not suitable for individuals with shellfish allergies. The combination of olive oil, garlic, mushrooms, and lime juice provides antioxidant and anti-inflammatory compounds relevant to rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular health contexts.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for camarones con hongos as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked shrimp and cooked mushrooms with olive oil adjustment, and are broadly consistent with the input record values. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-defined and the USDA component data is high quality.
notes: 1. Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (cooked shrimp FDC ID 175180; cooked white mushrooms FDC ID 169251) weighted at approximately 65% shrimp and 35% mushrooms with olive oil addition; no single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (3.8) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.4) are estimated from olive oil and shrimp fatty acid profiles per USDA FoodData Central; not directly measured for this dish. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — olive oil and shrimp contain no trans fats per USDA FoodData Central. 4. potassium_mg (320.0) is an estimate from shrimp and mushroom USDA component data; not directly measured for this dish. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite sautéed dish preparation are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16; component-level data exist but composite dish values were not aggregated to avoid compounding estimation error across multiple uncertain variables. 6. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables for low-carbohydrate mixed seafood and vegetable dishes; no direct GI measurement for camarones con hongos was found. 7. Input record listed country_of_origin as Singapore and ethnic_tags including Taco, Burrito, and Naan — these have been corrected; the dish is Mexican in origin and the irrelevant ethnic tags have been removed. 8. Input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) is a forbidden source (delivery/restaurant platform); replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the approved nutrient source. 9. Halal status is assumed based on shrimp and mushroom ingredients with olive oil; no pork or alcohol ingredients identified, but preparation environment cannot be verified from available data.
